source_paper_title: Algorithms for Competitive Division of Chores

Page 10, end of Related Work / start of Section 2 (statement just before Section 2), and reiterated in Section 3 (“What if both n and m are large?”), page 19.

Computing an exact competitive equilibrium in polynomial time when both the number of agents and chores are variable remains an open question for additive utilities.

problem. Is it possible to compute one competitive utility profile in time polynomial in \(n + m\)?
title: Compute a competitive equilibrium utility profile for divisible chores with variable size
background_latex:
There are \(n\) agents and \(m\) divisible chores, with one unit of each chore to be allocated. Agent \(i\in[n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\) receives a bundle \(z_i=(z_{i,1},\dots,z_{i,m})\in\mathbb{R}^m_{\ge 0}\), where \(z_{i,j}\) is the fraction of chore \(j\in[m]=\{1,\dots,m\}\) assigned to agent \(i\). Feasibility requires \(\sum_{i=1}^n z_{i,j}=1\) for every chore \(j\).

Preferences are additive and strictly negative (chores): values are given by a matrix \(v\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times m}_{<0}\), where \(v_{i,j}<0\) is agent \(i\)'s value for performing one unit of chore \(j\). The utility of agent \(i\) for bundle \(z_i\) is
\[u_i(z_i)=\sum_{j=1}^m v_{i,j} z_{i,j},\]
and the utility profile of an allocation \(z\) is \(u(z)=(u_1(z_1),\dots,u_n(z_n))\in\mathbb{R}^n_{<0}\).

Agents have (virtual) strictly negative budgets \(b\in\mathbb{R}^n_{<0}\). A price vector is \(p\in\mathbb{R}^m_{<0}\), and the price of a bundle \(x\in\mathbb{R}^m_{\ge 0}\) is \(p(x)=\sum_{j=1}^m p_j x_j\).

A feasible allocation \(z\) is a competitive allocation (competitive equilibrium with budgets \(b\)) if there exists \(p\in\mathbb{R}^m_{<0}\) such that each agent \(i\) maximizes \(u_i(x)\) over all bundles \(x\in\mathbb{R}^m_{\ge 0}\) satisfying \(p(x)\le b_i\). A competitive utility profile is any \(u\in\mathbb{R}^n\) for which there exists a competitive allocation \(z\) with \(u=u(z)\).

In the paper, when either \(n\) or \(m\) is fixed, there is a strongly polynomial-time algorithm that enumerates all competitive utility profiles for additive chores; however, the complexity when both \(n\) and \(m\) are variable is unresolved.
problem_definition_latex: Design an algorithm that, given arbitrary \(n,m\), a valuation matrix \(v\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times m}_{<0}\) and budgets \(b\in\mathbb{R}^n_{<0}\), computes at least one competitive utility profile \(u\in\mathbb{R}^n_{<0}\) (equivalently, one competitive allocation) in time polynomial in \(n+m\).

Agent \(i\in[n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\) receives a bundle \(z_i=(z_{i,1},\dots,z_{i,m})\in\mathbb{R}^m_{\ge 0}\), where \(z_{i,j}\) is the fraction of chore \(j\in[m]=\{1,\dots,m\}\) assigned to agent \(i\). Feasibility requires \(\sum_{i=1}^n z_{i,j}=1\) for every chore \(j\).

Preferences are additive and strictly negative (chores): values are given by a matrix \(v\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times m}_{<0}\), where \(v_{i,j}<0\) is agent \(i\)'s value for performing one unit of chore \(j\). The utility of agent \(i\) for bundle \(z_i\) is
\[u_i(z_i)=\sum_{j=1}^m v_{i,j} z_{i,j},\]
and the utility profile of an allocation \(z\) is \(u(z)=(u_1(z_1),\dots,u_n(z_n))\in\mathbb{R}^n_{<0}\).

Agents have (virtual) strictly negative budgets \(b\in\mathbb{R}^n_{<0}\). A price vector is \(p\in\mathbb{R}^m_{<0}\), and the price of a bundle \(x\in\mathbb{R}^m_{\ge 0}\) is \(p(x)=\sum_{j=1}^m p_j x_j\).

Design an algorithm that, given arbitrary \(n,m\), a valuation matrix \(v\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times m}_{<0}\) and budgets \(b\in\mathbb{R}^n_{<0}\), computes at least one competitive utility profile \(u\in\mathbb{R}^n_{<0}\) (equivalently, one competitive allocation) in time polynomial in \(n+m\).
location_in_paper: Page 10, end of Related Work / start of Section 2 (statement just before Section 2), and reiterated in Section 3 (“What if both n and m are large?”), page 19.
open_problem_quote:
Computing an exact competitive equilibrium in polynomial time when both the number of agents and chores are variable remains an open question for additive utilities.

problem. Is it possible to compute one competitive utility profile in time polynomial in \(n + m\)?
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The problem asks for an algorithmic upper bound (polynomial-time solvability) rather than a universal inequality or structural conjecture that could be refuted by a finite instance. While one could search for hard instances suggesting intractability, there is no well-defined finite certificate of impossibility without a complexity-theoretic reduction.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can represent candidate algorithms (e.g., pivoting rules, combinatorial procedures, or reductions to flow/LP subroutines) and test them computationally on random and adversarial instances, with a clear fitness signal (success/failure and runtime scaling). However, empirical performance does not certify worst-case polynomial time, so the feedback is only a heuristic proxy for the true goal.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem asks for a polynomial-time algorithm (in fully variable dimensions) that computes at least one competitive utility profile/allocation for divisible chores with additive strictly negative utilities and strictly negative budgets. Br\^anzei and Sandomirskiy show that when either the number of agents or chores is fixed, one can enumerate all competitive utility profiles by (i) enumerating a rich family of consumption graphs encoding faces of the Pareto frontier via maximal weighted welfare (MWW) graphs, (ii) deriving a candidate utility profile from a graph using explicit path-product formulas, and (iii) certifying competitiveness and recovering an allocation/prices via a max-flow test. Their work highlights that chores equilibria correspond to critical points of Nash social welfare on the Pareto frontier (non-convex, potentially disconnected), unlike the convex Eisenberg--Gale structure for goods.

For the fully variable case, exact polynomial-time computation of even one equilibrium remains unresolved in the additive, finite-disutility model. Subsequent work has made progress mainly on approximation and on broader models: Boodaghians--Chaudhury--Mehta give polynomial-time algorithms for approximate competitive equilibria for chores, while Chaudhury--Garg--McGlaughlin--Mehta develop combinatorial methods and hardness results in related settings and show NP-hardness of existence when infinite disutilities are permitted. Path-following methods (e.g., Lemke--Howson-type) for mixed manna provide additional algorithmic tools but currently do not yield a proven polynomial-time exact algorithm for additive chores with arbitrary \(n,m\). The main gap is thus an exact, dimension-polynomial algorithm that avoids enumerating exponentially many faces/utility profiles yet provably finds one competitive equilibrium.

sources.title: Competitive division of a mixed manna
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces and studies the competitive rule for mixed manna (goods and bads), proving existence and characterizing competitive allocations for chores as critical points of Nash social welfare on the feasible utility set; establishes fairness (envy-freeness) and efficiency (Pareto optimality) properties.
sources.connection: Provides the foundational equilibrium concept and geometric characterization (critical points of Nash product on the Pareto frontier) that motivates why computing a competitive equilibrium for chores is non-convex and algorithmically challenging.

sources.title: Dividing bads under additive utilities
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies competitive division for purely bads with additive utilities; analyzes structure of equilibria (including multiplicity) and provides insights for the two-agent case; shows exponential number of competitive utility profiles can occur when both n and m vary.
sources.connection: Gives key structural properties and lower bounds that frame the open complexity question for variable n and m, and motivates the need for algorithms that find at least one equilibrium without enumerating all profiles.

sources.title: Market equilibria in polynomial time for fixed number of goods or agents
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives polynomial-time algorithms for computing market equilibria when the number of goods or agents is fixed, using cell enumeration techniques to handle potentially disconnected equilibrium sets.
sources.connection: Provides a precedent for fixed-parameter tractability in non-convex/disconnected equilibrium settings and highlights the gap for fully variable dimensions, analogous to the chores equilibrium computation problem.

sources.title: Competitive allocation of a mixed manna
sources.theorem_or_result: Proposes a Lemke–Howson-type path-following algorithm for computing competitive allocations in mixed manna problems under separable piecewise-linear concave utilities; demonstrates practical performance.
sources.connection: Suggests algorithmic approaches (pivoting/path-following) that might be adapted to additive chores, though it does not yield a proven polynomial-time algorithm for the exact additive-chores case with variable n,m.

sources.title: Polynomial time algorithms to find an approximate competitive equilibrium for chores
sources.theorem_or_result: Designs polynomial-time algorithms for computing approximate competitive equilibria for chores (relaxations of exact CE) under additive disutilities, providing provable approximation guarantees.
sources.connection: Gives the strongest known polynomial-time guarantees in the fully variable (n,m) regime, but for approximation rather than exact computation of a competitive utility profile/allocation.

sources.title: Competitive equilibrium with chores: Combinatorial algorithm and hardness
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops combinatorial algorithms and hardness results for competitive equilibrium with chores in broader models; establishes computational barriers and identifies tractable/approximable regimes.
sources.connection: Clarifies complexity landscape around chores equilibria and provides algorithmic ideas, but does not resolve exact polynomial-time computation for additive chores with finite disutilities when both n and m vary.

sources.title: On the existence of competitive equilibrium with chores
sources.theorem_or_result: Shows that when agents may have infinite disutilities, competitive equilibrium may fail to exist and deciding existence is NP-hard.
sources.connection: Demonstrates that extending the model (allowing infinite disutilities) makes even existence hard, underscoring why the exact polynomial-time computation question is delicate; however the open problem here assumes strictly negative finite values where existence holds.
